grammar.usage: Habits, routines, permanent situations, general truths, state verbs
grammar.example: My mum calls me every weekend without fail.

grammar.usage: Actions happening now, temporary situations, changing situations, annoying habits
grammar.example: Dan is talking on the phone right now.

grammar.usage: Past action with present result, situations starting in the past continuing to present, completed actions at unspecified time
grammar.example: I've got a splitting headache thanks to you.

grammar.usage: Actions continuing up to now, temporary situations, actions that just stopped and have a present result
grammar.example: I've been travelling round France all summer.

blocks.content: 1. STRUCTURE OF PRESENT SIMPLE & PRESENT CONTINUOUS

rows:
S + V1/es (+ O)
EX: My mum calls me every weekend without fail.

S + am/is/are + Ving (+ O)
EX: Dan is talking on the phone right now.

S + do / does + NOT + Vbare (+ O)
EX: Minh doesn't talk over the phone very often.

S + am / is / are + NOT + Ving (+ O)
EX: We aren't eating any meal at the moment as we are on diet.

Do / does + S + Vbare (+ O)
EX: Does train stop at Manchester on Sundays?

Am/Is/Are + S + Ving (+ O)
EX: Is air travel getting increasingly safe?

* Can use "do/does" in affirmative sentences for emphasis
EX: You don't like going by bus, do you?
→ Actually, I do like going by bus for short distance!

* Do not use stative verbs (e.g. be, have, imagine, look, see, smell, taste, think) when they express states.
* Note: Some of these verbs can be used in the continuous form when they have an active meaning (e.g. have dinner, look at, etc.)

blocks.content: 3. USAGE OF PRESENT SIMPLE & PRESENT CONTINUOUS

rows:
- Habits
- Repeated actions
- Permanent situations
- Present states
- General truths

- Actions happening now
- Temporary series of actions
- Temporary situations
- Changing and developing situations
- Annoying habits (usually with 'always')

EX: You're always coming up with excuse for not having done homework.

richGrammar.title: B) PRESENT PERFECT SIMPLE & PRESENT PERFECT CONTINUOUS

blocks.content: 1. STRUCTURE OF PRESENT PERFECT SIMPLE & PRESENT PERFECT CONTINUOUS

rows:
S + have/has + V3/ed (+ O)
EX: I've got a splitting headache thanks to you.

S + have/has + been + Ving (+ O)
EX: I've been travelling round France all summer.

S + have/has + NOT + V3/ed (+ O)
EX: He hasn't finished the book yet so I can't tell you what happens.

S + have / has + NOT + been + Ving (+ O)
EX: He hasn't been playing games with me in a year.

Have / Has + S + V3/ed (+ O)
EX: Have you ever heard this funny story?

Have / Has + S + been + Ving (+ O)
EX: Has Jenny been living here for the last six years?

* Use with "It's the first/second/etc time"
EX: It's the first time I've ever flown in a helicopter.

* US vs. UK Usage differences:
- US: Often use Past Simple (e.g. We already saw the Sphinx.) and "gotten" as past participle (e.g. We've already gotten Dan a new backpack.)
- UK: Often use Present Perfect (e.g. We've already seen the Sphinx.) and "got" as past participle (e.g. We've already got Dan a new backpack.)

* Often used with time expressions like: all day/week/year, for, since, just, etc.
* Do not use with "ever" and "never" in this tense.
- ✓ Have you ever flown in a helicopter before?
- ✗ Have you ever been flying in a helicopter before?
* Sometimes there is very little difference in meaning between the simple and continuous forms.

blocks.content: 3. USAGE OF PRESENT PERFECT SIMPLE & PRESENT PERFECT CONTINUOUS

rows:
- Past situations with present results
- Situations continuing from the past to the present
- Completed actions at an unspecified time
- Actions completed and still affecting the present

- Actions continuing up to the present
- Actions that have just finished and have a result now
- Temporary situations or series of actions

EX: I'm out of breath because I've been running to get here in time.

bullets: Stative verbs are verbs that express a state rather than an action. They are often related to: thoughts/opinions, emotions, appearance, possession & relationship, existence, or senses.

Stative verbs are generally not used in continuous tenses.
- ✓ I see what you mean.
- ✗ I am seeing what you mean.
Some stative verbs (be, have, imagine, look, see, smell, taste, think) can be used in continuous tenses when they describe an action rather than a state.

EX1: Do you have your plane ticket with you? (meaning: possession - state)
EX2: Are you having lunch at the moment? (meaning: eating - action)

vocabulary.meaning: definitely; without exception or failure
vocabulary.example: He promised to call me at noon without fail.

vocabulary.meaning: with the exclusion of; besides
vocabulary.example: Apart from the weather, the holiday was perfect.

vocabulary.meaning: to establish communication with someone
vocabulary.example: Please get in touch with me as soon as you arrive.

vocabulary.word: stay in touch / keep in touch / be in touch
vocabulary.meaning: to maintain contact with someone
vocabulary.example: We promised to keep in touch after graduation.

vocabulary.meaning: to defeat someone or make them unconscious in a competition
vocabulary.example: The champion knocked his opponent out of the tournament.

vocabulary.meaning: a game played on an ice rink or field where two teams use curved sticks to hit a puck or ball into a goal
vocabulary.example: He plays ice hockey every winter.

vocabulary.meaning: not often; seldom
vocabulary.example: She rarely eats fast food.

vocabulary.meaning: a pilot or flying machine from ancient times
vocabulary.example: Legends tell of ancient aviators flying over the mountains.

vocabulary.meaning: a highly developed human society with its own social, political, and cultural organization
vocabulary.example: The Nile Valley was the home of an ancient civilization.

vocabulary.meaning: left behind or far outperformed; covered in dust
vocabulary.example: The runner left all his competitors in the dust.

vocabulary.meaning: a strange, unusual, or unconventional explanation or idea
vocabulary.example: He proposed a bizarre theory about time travel.

vocabulary.meaning: a very severe pain in the head
vocabulary.example: I've got a splitting headache and need to lie down.

vocabulary.meaning: a scientist whose views are accepted by the majority of the scientific community
vocabulary.example: His ideas were rejected by mainstream scientists.

vocabulary.meaning: it is not worth doing because it won't achieve anything
vocabulary.example: There is no point in arguing with him.

vocabulary.meaning: because of; expected or scheduled to arrive
vocabulary.example: The flight was delayed due to bad weather.

vocabulary.meaning: to speak quietly and unclearly
vocabulary.example: She mumbled something I couldn't hear.

vocabulary.meaning: to say or think that someone or something is responsible for something bad
vocabulary.example: Don't blame me for your mistakes.

vocabulary.meaning: used to emphasize a comparison
vocabulary.example: This is by far the best book I've read.

vocabulary.meaning: an unexpected event or fact
vocabulary.example: His sudden visit was a pleasant surprise.

vocabulary.meaning: to look like or be similar to another person or thing
vocabulary.example: The baby strongly resembles her father.

vocabulary.meaning: impossible or very difficult to believe; extremely good
vocabulary.example: The view from the top was incredible.

vocabulary.meaning: without doubt; clearly or surely
vocabulary.example: I will definitely come to your party.

vocabulary.meaning: being uncertain or undecided
vocabulary.example: The future of the project is in doubt.

vocabulary.meaning: a large amount or number of something
vocabulary.example: I have loads of work to do today.

vocabulary.meaning: to suggest or think of an idea or plan
vocabulary.example: She came up with a brilliant idea for the project.

vocabulary.meaning: a group of people who are friendly with each other
vocabulary.example: She has a wide circle of friends at school.

vocabulary.meaning: an exciting or unusual experience; to go on an adventure
vocabulary.example: They went on a wild adventure in the jungle.

vocabulary.meaning: willing to try new and often difficult or dangerous things
vocabulary.example: She is an adventurous traveler who loves exploring.
